title: Lists — Creating and Accessing
subtitle: Build and read your first Python lists
learning_objectives: Create a list using square bracket syntax
Access list elements by positive and negative index
Use len() to count elements in a list
Check whether a value exists in a list with the in operator

sections.heading: Creating a List
sections.content: A list is an ordered, mutable collection of items enclosed in square brackets []. Items are separated by commas. Lists can hold any type — strings, integers, floats, booleans, or even a mix of types in the same list.
sections.code_example:
# A list of strings
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

# A list of integers
scores = [95, 88, 72, 100]

# A mixed-type list
mixed = ["Alice", 30, True, 3.14]

print(fruits)
print(scores)
print(mixed)

sections.heading: Accessing Elements by Index
sections.content: Every item in a list has a numeric position called an index, starting at 0. Use list[index] to retrieve an element. Python also supports negative indexing: -1 refers to the last element, -2 to the second-to-last, and so on.
sections.code_example:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

# Positive indexing
print(fruits[0])   # first element
print(fruits[1])   # second element
print(fruits[2])   # third element

# Negative indexing
print(fruits[-1])  # last element
print(fruits[-2])  # second to last

sections.heading: List Length and Membership
sections.content: Use the built-in len() function to find how many items a list contains. Use the in operator to check whether a specific value is present in the list — this returns True or False.
sections.code_example:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

# Count elements
print(len(fruits))          # 3

# Membership check
print("apple" in fruits)    # True
print("mango" in fruits)    # False
print("mango" not in fruits) # True

exercises.title: Build a Grocery List
exercises.prompt: Create a list called grocery that contains exactly three items: "milk", "eggs", and "bread" (in that order). Print the entire list.
exercises.starter_code:
# Create a list called grocery containing "milk", "eggs", and "bread"

# Print the list
hints: Make sure the items are strings wrapped in double or single quotes

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors

exercises.title: First and Last
exercises.prompt: Given the list colors = ["red", "green", "blue", "yellow"], print the first element using index 0 and the last element using index -1.
exercises.starter_code:
colors = ["red", "green", "blue", "yellow"]

# Print the first element

# Print the last element
hints: Use colors[0] for the first item
Use colors[-1] for the last item — negative indexes count from the end

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors
The output should contain 'red' (the first element)
The output should contain 'yellow' (the last element)

exercises.title: Membership and Length
exercises.prompt: Given animals = ["cat", "dog", "rabbit"], print the number of animals using len(), then print whether "dog" is in the list and whether "fish" is in the list.
exercises.starter_code:
animals = ["cat", "dog", "rabbit"]

# Print the number of animals

# Check if "dog" is in the list

# Check if "fish" is in the list
exercises.solution:
animals = ["cat", "dog", "rabbit"]
print(len(animals))
print("dog" in animals)
print("fish" in animals)
hints: len(animals) returns the count as an integer
Use the 'in' keyword: "dog" in animals returns True or False

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors
The output should contain '3' (the length of the list)
The output should contain 'True' for 'dog' in animals
The output should contain 'False' for 'fish' in animals

quiz.question: Given the list nums = [10, 20, 30, 40], what does nums[-1] return?
quiz.explanation: Negative index -1 always refers to the last element of the list. Here that is 40.

quiz.question: Which of the following creates a valid Python list containing three strings?
quiz.explanation: Lists use square brackets []. Parentheses create a tuple, curly braces create a set or dict, and a bare comma-separated sequence is just a tuple without parentheses.

completion_message: Great work! You can now create lists, access elements by index (including negative indexes), measure their length, and test for membership. These are the building blocks for every list operation ahead.
